text:
Admission Date:  2000-10-19              Discharge Date:   2010-03-07

Date of Birth:  1995-08-20             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Sarah
Chief Complaint:
trauma after high speed Tammy

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
R acetabular repair
intra-arterial TPA
PEG tube placement
PICC placement


History of Present Illness:
The pt is a 58 year-old man with PMH of HTN and alcoholism who
was admitted to the Trauma SICU after a high speed Cory Turner MD (pt
inebriated) on 2000-10-19. The patient's ICU course was
complicated by delirium tremens and a ck/troponin leak that was
attributed to cardiac contusion or demand ischemia. He had
repair of a right acetabular fracture in the OR in 04-18. He
left the OR at 12:30. He slept until 4pm when he was noted to
have left facial droop, left sided weakness and difficulty
producing speech. Found to have R MCA infarction. Underwent
intraarterial TPA and readmitted to ICU. There, developed MSSA
infection of R lateral knee pin site as well as MSSA bilateral
pneumonia. ICU course also marked by delirium. Was transferred
to floor for further care on 1974-06-09 for further care.

Past Medical History:
HTN
ETOH abuse
Hyperlipidemia
oral cancer s/p resection
known lung nodules awaiting further work-up

Social History:
ETOH use - had positive alcohol level on admission. 135.
Tobacco - Per pacu nurse pt quit smoking 3 years ago.


Family History:
NA

Physical Exam:
Vitals: T:100.3 P:85 R:15 BP:120/55 SaO2:100% on 3 L.
General: Awake, cooperative, NAD.
Neurologic:
-[**Date Range 4189**] Status: Alert, dysarthric, follows commands to open eyes
and close hands.
-Cranial Nerves: Fixed right gaze deviation. Left visual field
cut. Patient keeps eyes closed most of the time. Facial droop on
the left is subtle at rest. Smiling brings out the left facial
droop. Forehead wrinkling equal bilaterally. Hearing intact to
commands.
-Motor: Right arm is normal (raises for 10 seconds). Right leg
post surgical. Left arm is 0/5. Left leg is 11-03.
-Sensory: Patient was unable to detect noxious stimuli on the
left arm and leg.
-Coordination: Patient able to touch hand without dysmetria or
ataxia with the right hand.
-DTRs: Not tested.  Plantar response not tested.
-Gait: Not tested.

1981-08-24 1:02 pm SWAB      RIGHT THIGH ABSCESS.

                            **FINAL REPORT 1974-06-09**

   GRAM STAIN (Final 1981-08-24):

LEUKOCYTES.
      3+  (5-10 per 1000X FIELD):   GRAM POSITIVE COCCI.
                                    IN PAIRS AND CLUSTERS.

   WOUND CULTURE (Final 2009-04-14):
      STAPH AUREUS COAG +.    HEAVY GROWTH
.
2003-07-04 10:06 am SPUTUM      Source: Induced.

                            **FINAL REPORT 2009-04-14**

   GRAM STAIN (Final 2003-07-04):
      >25 PMNs and <10 epithelial cells/100X field.
      4+   (>10 per 1000X FIELD):   GRAM POSITIVE COCCI.
                                    IN PAIRS AND CLUSTERS.

   RESPIRATORY CULTURE (Final 2009-04-14):

      STAPH AUREUS COAG +.    MODERATE GROWTH.

03-18 CT CHEST/ABD/PELV
IMPRESSION:
1. Right posterior-lateral acetabular fracture as described
above with associated posterior dislocation of the right femoral
head. Intramuscular and intra-articular hematoma at the right
hip.
2. Multiple left minimally displaced left anterior rib
fractures.
3. Two 4 mm nodules within the right upper lobe. According to
the Anderson Society guidance in a low risk patient, no
further followup is necessary.
4. Focal ground-glass opacity within the right middle lobe may
represent lung contusion.
5. Extensive atherosclerotic changes of the aorta without
evidence of aneurysm.
.
2000-10-19
CT C-SPINE W/O CONTRAST
IMPRESSION:
No acute fracture or malalignment. Degenerative changes as
described above.
.
2000-10-19 CT HEAD W/O CONTRAST
IMPRESSION: No acute intracranial pathology
.
2016-07-18 CTA HEAD W&W/O C & RECONS; CTA NECK W&W/OC & RECONS
IMPRESSION:
1. CT head demonstrates early infarct in the right middle
cerebral artery territory.
2. Perfusion CT demonstrates delayed transit time in the right
middle cerebral artery region with a slightly smaller blood
volume defect indicating infarct with ischemic penumbra.
3. CTA of the head demonstrates high-grade stenosis/occlusion at
the right middle cerebral artery bifurcation with diminished
sylvian branches. A small 3-mm aneurysm is also suspected at the
anterior communicating artery. Correlation with angiography is
recommended.
4. Calcification at both carotid bifurcations without high-grade
stenosis. Normal appearance of the vertebral arteries.
.
1975-10-20 INTRAARTERIAL TPA
IMPRESSION: Christopher Andrews underwent cerebral arteriography and
mechanical and IV TPA thrombolysis of the right M1 segment with
recanalization of all divisions except one M2 branch,
corresponding to the superior division of the right middle
cerebral artery. He tolerated the procedure well and there were
no complications.
.
2012-01-29
TTE:
No PFO/ASD seen. Mild symmetric left ventricular hypetrophy with
preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function.
Mild pulmonary hypertension.

2012-01-29 Head CT:
Evolution of right MCA infarct, which is now better visualized.
A small hyperdensity which appears to be due to petechial
hemorrhage is also visualized. No large hematoma or midline
shift.
.
1981-08-24 CAROTID SERIES COMPLETE
IMPRESSION:
Less than 40% stenosis, right ICA and 40-59% stenosis left ICA.
.
1978-11-15
PELVIS (AP ONLY)
FINDINGS: Extensive metallic fixation devices are seen about the
previous fracture of the right acetabulum. No evidence of
hardware-related complication. Of incidental note are extensive
calcifications in the aorta and iliofemoral regions.


Brief Hospital Course:
After his high speed motor vehicle accident, Mr. Andrews was
initially admitted to the Meyers trauma service. He was found to
have a R posterior column posterior wall acetabular fracture
with associated hip dislocation. A closed reduction was
attempted but the reduction was unable to be maintained. He was
therefore placed in skeletal traction. On 02-13 he was
transfered to the ICU for agitation felt to be secondary to
DT's. The traction failed to reduce the fracture. Hence, he
underwent ORIF of the posterior wall/posterior colummn on 04-18,
which was successful.
.
In the PACU, several hours after his ORIF, he was noted to have
left sided facial droop, left arm > leg weakness, and
dysarthria. Was out of window for IV TPA. Taken for stat
CTA/CT-perfusion that demonstrated occlusion of the right MCA at
the end of M1. He was then taken to neurointerventional
angiography urgently for merci thrombus retrieval w/ IA tpa.
Transferred to neuro ICU for closer monitoring.
.
On 12-21, he was transferred back to the floor for further care.
A stroke workup (for embolic source), including carotid dopplers
and an echocardiogram, was negative. On 07-19 he was transferred
back to T-SICU for increasing respiratory distress, tachypnea,
and fever to 101.2. A sputum cx grew MSSA. CXR with new
consolidation  bilaterally in lower lungs. He was started on
vancomycin on 07-19. On 09-24 he developed purulence from lateral
traction pin site, a culture of which also grew MSSA. Vancomycin
was continued pending culture results. His [**Month/Day (4) **] status was
waxing and Sanchez throughout, c/w delirium.
.
On 11-22 he was transferred to the medicine floors for further
care. He was afebrile and vitals were stable but he was
delirious and required restraints and a 1:1 sitter. He
self-d/c'ed his NGT on multiple occasions. He was started on qHS
low dose antipsychotics (initially olazapine, changes to
seroquel by discharge). Since both his pneumonia and surgical
wound had grown MSSA, he was switched from vancomycin to
nafcillin on 1974-06-09. Over the next few days his [**Date Range **] status
slowly began to clear. In the meantime he remained afebrile and
without clinical signs of infection.
.
Since he was NPO and had no NGT, he was maintained on IV fluids
and GI was consulted for PEG placement to maintain nutrition in
post-stroke setting. A PEG was placed on 1972-12-26 and tube feeds
were started on PEG day 2. There were no problems with feedings
and he was easily titrated up to goal of 65cc/hr per nutrition
recs.
.
[**Date Range 1957**] continued to follow and felt no need to return him to the
OR for wound I+D. Rather, since the wound infection was
improving, it was recommended to continue Middlesex Hospital packing and
dressing changes, tapering the length of packing by 1 inch per
week. Surveillence pelvic films were taken on 05-23 and again on
06-30 after a minor fall, and showed the hardware to be in
correct position with no complications. Physical and
occupational therapy continued to work with the patient and
recommended rehab.
.
Mr. Andrews Mills status continued to improve daily, and he was
off restraints for the last 5 days of his admission. He did not
require a sitter after 2001-12-28. He did not attempt to d/c any
of his lines. At discharge he was a+o x 3, pleasant, and
interactive, although still moderately dysarthric due to his
stroke.
.
In anticipation of rehab placement, a right upper extremity PICC
line was placed on 05-23, again without complication. His IV
antibiotic course lasts until 1978-10-10.
.
Although he was clinically asymptomatic, Mr. Andrews' lipase was
chronically elevated and seemed to be exacerbated by tube
feeding. The gastroenterology service consulted and recommended
imaging (CT or MRCP) as outpatient to better evaluate and rule
out any pseudocysts or ductal obstruction.
.
Mr. Andrews also had mildly elevated blood glucose levels. He
did not carry a diagnosis of diabetes upon admission to the
hospital. It is hard to judge his true fasting glucose level due
to his tube feeds. This may be impaired glucose tolerance vs.
diabetes. He may have some pancreatic insufficiency from his
chronic EtOH abuse. We checked fingerstick glucose values qid
and covered Mr. Andrews with an insulin sliding scale. His
fingersticks were very rarely above 150, and generally in the
120-140 range. We recommend outpatient followup with his PCP for
formal diabetic testing.
.
Another outpatient issue concerns Mr. Andrews Mills function.
In the ICU his TSH was elevated and he was briefly started on
levothyroxine, however in the ICU setting the TSH is notoriously
unreliable, and a free Mills horome level the subsequent day
was normal. This most likely represented sick euthyroid
syndrome. As an outpatient, his provider can repeat [**Name9 (PRE) 29954**] at a
later date to better assess for true [**Name9 (PRE) **] disfunction.
.
Mr. Andrews is discharged on lovenox for post-hip fracture DVT
prophylaxis, which he should receive until 1975-10-21. He is
strictly NPO, with all feeds and pills via G tube, until cleared
by speech and swallow to advance his diet. He has orthopedic and
neurological followup scheduled. He was full code throughout his
hospitalization.

Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Baptist Medical Center South

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary: R acetabular fracture
.
Secondary:
Stroke with L-sided deficit
DT's
staph pneumonia
staph surgical wound infection
Anemia
mild pancreatitis
Reactive thrombocytosis
impaired glucose tolerance vs. diabetes

Discharge Condition:
Stable, improved


Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital after a high-speed car
accident in which you suffered a badly broken right hip and
thigh. After your surgery to repair these fractures, you
suffered from alcohol withdrawal. You also suffered a stroke
which impaired your ability to speak or swallow safely. In order
to feed you while you recover from the stroke, we placed a
feeding tube through your stomach to supply you with nutrition.
Finally, you suffered 2 hospital-acquired infections - one in
your surgical wound site, and the other in your lungs. We
treated you with antibiotics for these infections.
.
You are being discharged to rehab where you will continue to
rehabilitate your surgically repaired right hip as well as the
muscles which became weak after your stroke. You will also
finish your course of antibiotics while you are there.
.
1. Please take all medications as prescribed
2. Please call your doctor or come to the closest ED if you
develop new symptoms that re concerning such as new weakness or
numbness on one side of your body, worsened slurring of your
speech, fevers/chills, or shortness of breath.
